questions.question: In one or two sentences, define the copy-on-write technique.
keyPoints: A resource management optimization where a copy of a resource is deferred until a process attempts to modify it.
Before modification, processes share the same physical copy of the resource to save memory and reduce overhead.

questions.question: Explain the primary advantage of copy-on-write and describe a common scenario where an operating system uses it.
keyPoints: The primary advantage is efficient memory usage and performance improvement by avoiding unnecessary data copies.
A common scenario is the fork() system call, where the child process initially shares the parent's address space pages.
Copy-on-write occurs only when either process modifies a shared page, at which point a private copy is created.

questions.question: Describe in detail the step-by-step process of copy-on-write as implemented for a fork() system call. Include the roles of page tables, MMU, and the operating system kernel in handling a page fault.
keyPoints: Initially, after fork(), the parent and child's page table entries point to the same physical pages, marked as read-only.
When either process attempts to write to a shared page, the MMU triggers a page fault due to the read-only permission.
The OS kernel's page fault handler identifies this as a copy-on-write fault.
The kernel then allocates a new physical page, copies the original content to it, and updates the faulting process's page table to point to this new copy with read-write permissions.
The original page remains read-only for the other process, and the instruction that caused the fault is restarted.
